_comment: Platform-keyed risk map for system directories. Used by NavigatorTab to show warnings when the user navigates into sensitive areas. Risk levels: high = OS-critical, medium = system-managed (user data may not be there), low = typically safe but unusual for regular work.

/.label: Filesystem root
/.reason: Modifying files directly under / can render macOS unbootable.

/bin.label: System binaries
/bin.reason: Core macOS commands live here. Deletion or modification breaks the OS.

/sbin.label: System administration binaries
/sbin.reason: Low-level system tools. Modification may prevent startup or networking.

/usr.label: Unix system resources
/usr.reason: Contains system libraries and tools. Managed by macOS — do not modify.

/usr/bin.label: User-accessible system binaries
/usr/bin.reason: macOS system commands. Managed by SIP on modern macOS.

/usr/sbin.label: System administration binaries
/usr/sbin.reason: macOS system tools. Managed by SIP on modern macOS.

/usr/lib.label: System libraries
/usr/lib.reason: Shared libraries required by system applications.

/System.label: Core macOS system files
/System.reason: Protected by System Integrity Protection (SIP). Modification may require disabling SIP and can break macOS.

/System/Library.label: macOS system library
/System/Library.reason: Core OS frameworks and resources. SIP-protected on modern macOS.

/Library.label: System-wide application support
/Library.reason: Shared preferences and extensions for all users. Changes affect every account.

/Library/LaunchDaemons.label: System-wide launch daemons
/Library/LaunchDaemons.reason: Services that run as root at startup. Unauthorized additions are a common malware vector.

/Library/LaunchAgents.label: System-wide launch agents
/Library/LaunchAgents.reason: Services that run at login for all users. Review carefully before adding anything here.

/private/etc.label: System configuration files
/private/etc.reason: hosts, sudoers, ssh config, and other critical system settings live here.

/etc.label: System configuration files (symlink to /private/etc)
/etc.reason: Critical system configuration. Changes here affect the entire OS.

/var.label: Variable system data (symlink to /private/var)
/var.reason: Logs, caches, mail spools. Usually safe to read, careful with writes.

/tmp.label: Temporary files
/tmp.reason: Cleared on reboot. Safe for scratch work, but do not store anything important here.

/Applications.label: Installed applications
/Applications.reason: Moving or deleting apps from here uninstalls them for all users.

/dev.label: Device files
/dev.reason: Writing to device files can corrupt disks or cause data loss.

/Volumes.label: Mounted volumes
/Volumes.reason: External drives, disk images. Modifying files here affects the mounted media.

/.label: Filesystem root
/.reason: Modifying files directly under / can render Linux unbootable.

/bin.label: Essential system binaries
/bin.reason: Core commands (ls, cp, bash). Removing these makes the system unusable.

/sbin.label: System administration binaries
/sbin.reason: Boot and repair tools. Required for system startup.

/usr.label: User system resources
/usr.reason: Most installed software lives here. Managed by the package manager.

/usr/bin.label: User-accessible system binaries
/usr/bin.reason: Commands installed by the OS and package manager.

/usr/sbin.label: System administration binaries
/usr/sbin.reason: Admin tools installed by the OS.

/usr/lib.label: System libraries
/usr/lib.reason: Shared libraries required by installed software.

/usr/local.label: Locally installed software
/usr/local.reason: Software installed outside the package manager. Safer than /usr, but changes affect all users.

/etc.label: System configuration files
/etc.reason: Every daemon, service, and system setting is configured here. Mistakes can break networking, authentication, or boot.

/etc/passwd.label: User account database
/etc/passwd.reason: Corrupting this file can lock all users out of the system.

/etc/shadow.label: Hashed passwords
/etc/shadow.reason: Contains password hashes for all accounts. Root-only access.

/etc/sudoers.label: sudo permissions
/etc/sudoers.reason: Controls who can run commands as root. Syntax errors lock out sudo entirely.

/etc/ssh.label: SSH configuration
/etc/ssh.reason: Host keys and daemon config. Changes here affect remote access security.

/boot.label: Boot files
/boot.reason: Kernel, initrd, and bootloader config. Corruption here makes the system unbootable.

/dev.label: Device files
/dev.reason: Writing to device files can corrupt disks or cause data loss.

/proc.label: Process and kernel information (virtual)
/proc.reason: Virtual filesystem exposing kernel state. Safe to read, some writes change kernel parameters.

/sys.label: Kernel and hardware interface (virtual)
/sys.reason: Writing to /sys entries can change hardware behavior or crash the system.

/lib.label: Essential shared libraries
/lib.reason: Libraries required by /bin and /sbin. Removal prevents the system from booting.

/root.label: Root user's home directory
/root.reason: The superuser's home. Requires root access. Changes affect system-level configurations.

/var.label: Variable data
/var.reason: Logs, spools, caches, and databases. Safe to read; be careful deleting — some apps depend on this data.

/var/log.label: System logs
/var/log.reason: Deleting logs removes your audit trail. Writing to log files can confuse monitoring tools.

/tmp.label: Temporary files
/tmp.reason: Cleared on reboot. Safe for scratch work, but do not store anything important here.

/opt.label: Optional/third-party software
/opt.reason: Common install location for non-packaged software. Changes here affect all users.

/mnt.label: Temporary mount point
/mnt.reason: Used to manually mount external drives or network shares.

/media.label: Auto-mounted removable media
/media.reason: USB drives, CD-ROMs. Modifying files here affects the mounted media.

/snap.label: Snap package mount points
/snap.reason: Managed by snapd. Modifications may be overwritten by the package manager.
